content:
Most developers think AI will replace junior engineers first.

8 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

After working through multiple AI-assisted projects, I think that’s the wrong framing.
The bigger risk is becoming the engineer who only knows how to write code manually while the rest of the team learns how to build, debug, and operate AI-enhanced systems.
A year ago, one of our teams shipped a feature in three days that would have taken two weeks previously. The difference wasn’t that we hired more people. The difference was that two engineers understood how to use AI effectively for code generation, test creation, documentation analysis, and debugging. The other engineers spent most of their time fighting the tools, reviewing low-quality AI output, and fixing mistakes introduced by prompts that looked reasonable but produced dangerous code.
The expensive mistake isn’t ignoring AI completely. It’s assuming that knowing how to ask ChatGPT for code is an AI skill.
It isn’t.
By 2027, the developers who will have the most leverage are the ones who understand how AI systems behave, how to integrate them safely into production workflows, and how to evaluate their output with engineering judgment.
